THE NGA SOFTWARE WAY ELEMENTS

To consistently track these metrics and realize the above intent, the rest of this document provides a set of elements NGA and contractor teams must meet while delivering software products. These metrics and elements, collectively referred to as "The NGA Software Way," ensure each software product has the people, processes, and tools in place to effectively operate at NGA. They apply to any team building a new product or modifying one already in service, whether making small changes, adding large new features, or creating entirely new products. The NGA Software Way applies to software products of all phases, including existing software products across the agency (not simply new products). Except where noted below, these elements must also be used when evaluating and implementing commercial software products for use at NGA. We understand that for large, existing software products, implementing The NGA Software Way won't happen all at once; it should start with tracking the metrics above and iteratively meeting each of the elements below.

3. Prioritize Rapidly Delivering Value to Users

Once user needs are deeply understood, the team must decide which of those needs to prioritize. To do this, the team must define a minimum viable product (MVP), either in written form or, ideally, as a set of wireframes, and then share it with key stakeholders, customers, and users. The MVP is the smallest actual deliverable that can be reviewed by real customers to get feedback on if it solves a need and to test assumptions, learn quickly, and decrease risk upfront. The product manager must break down the MVP into actionable work items that are easily understood by the entire team. Teams must gather feedback as early as possible using mockups or interactive prototypes. As feedback is gathered, the prototype's fidelity should mature, and the team must transition to testing with the real product.

Each day a product isn't actively solving user needs in production increases the risk the team is building the wrong thing. Teams must prioritize rapidly getting a product to actual users in production to learn what works and how to successfully iterate; this first capability must be delivered in months, not years.

In accordance with FAR 15.503(a)(2), a SPECIAL NOTICE providing preaward notice to small business offerors will be posted to the GPE (SAM.gov) today, 5 March 2026, prior to the award of contracts pursuant to the CASS acquisition, which is anticipated for Wednesday, 11 March 2026. When using a small business set-aside, as here (see FAR Subpart 19.5), "the contracting officer shall notify each offeror in writing prior to award . . . ." FAR 15.503(a)(2). This preaward notification for small businesses allows small business offerors an opportunity to protest an apparent successful offeror's small-business size representation prior to award of a contract or immediately thereafter. See FAR 19.302(d). A size protest filed prior to notice being made of the selection of the apparent successful offeror(s) would be dismissed as premature by the Small Business Administration (SBA). FAR 19.302(d)(4). Upon receipt of the SPECIAL NOTICE identifying the apparent successful offeror(s), any concern or other interested party may file a protest with the contracting officer; however, in order to be considered "timely" it must be received by the contracting officer by the close of business of the fifth business day after the SPECIAL NOTICE is received. See FAR 19.302(d)(1). A protest must be timely in order to affect a specific solicitation. FAR 19.302(d).

52.219-6 Notice of Total Small Business Set-Aside. (Deviation 2026-O0038) Feb 2026

The small business size standard for a concern that submits an offer, other than on a construction or service acquisition, but proposes to furnish an end item that it did not itself manufacture, process, or produce (i.e., nonmanufacturer), is 500 employees, or 150 employees for information technology value-added resellers under NAICS code 541519, if the acquisition—
(i) Is set aside for small business and has a value above the simplified acquisition threshold;
(ii) Uses the HUBZone price evaluation preference regardless of dollar value, unless the offeror waives the price evaluation preference; or
(iii) Is an 8(a), HUBZone, service-disabled veteran-owned, economically disadvantaged women-owned, or women-owned small business set-aside or sole-source award regardless of dollar value.

The NAICS code for this acquisition is 541511. The small business size standard is $34.0M.

This procurement is reserved 100% for Small Businesses. The associated North American Industrial Classification System (NAICS) code for this requirement is 541612, Human Resource Consulting Services, and the small business size standard is $29,000,000.00 dollars. Offerors must be registered in System of Award Management (SAM) database https://www.sam.gov/SAM/.

EVALUATION of the technical approach will be based upon the extent to which the vendor thoroughly and accurately describes the overall technical solution. It is the Government's intention to issue a single firm-fixed price contract.

The Government will award a single contract resulting from this request for quotes to the responsible vendor whose quote, conforming to the RFQ; will be most advantageous to the Government, price and other factors considered.

The Contracting Officer reserves the right to evaluate quotes without the use of formal evaluation procedures such as establishing a competitive range, conducting discussions, scoring quotations, and to make an award to other than the lowest quote.

To evaluate price, quotes will be evaluated using a price/technical Tradeoff. This process is appropriate because it is in the Government's best interest to consider award to other than the lowest price. In evaluating the technical portion of the quote, the technical evaluators will evaluate the following factors, Technical, Past Performance and Price. The factors of Technical Capability and Past Performance are weighted equally. All evaluation factors other than price, when combined, are significantly more important than price.

This acquisition is set-aside for small business concerns. This solicitation incorporates provisions and clauses by reference. The full text of provisions and clauses may be accessed electronically at www.acquisition.gov.

The applicable North American Industry Classification Standard Code is 541611. This solicitation is being issued as 100% Small Business Set Aside. All responsible sources may submit a quotation which will be considered by the agency.

Anticipated Date of Occupancy/Period of Performance is June 1, 2026 – May 31, 2027, with two possible option years.

Statement of Requirement
The horticulture specialist will assist RMA personnel and other interested parties to provide advice on nursery issues related to subtropical and temperate plants in areas covered by the 10 RMA Regional Offices. The Contractor shall perform the following tasks as detailed below:
(i) Advise, assist, and coordinate with RMA personnel and other interested parties on questions and issues related to nursery plant production practices, plant classification and taxonomy, hardiness zone limitations, storage and plant protection procedures and requirements, risk assessment, wholesale marketing, and nursery crop insurance procedures.
(ii) Attend RMA and nursery industry meetings, as necessary.
(iii) Aid and participate with RMA personnel in large claims, as necessary.
(iv) Possess knowledge of the NVS pilot crop insurance program to advise and assist RMA in modifications to the nursery program.

(A) Technical Proposal: Offerors are requested to submit an oral technical proposal presentation and a price proposal in response to the attached Performance Work Statement (PWS) (See Attachment 1). The Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan is provided for contractor reference (See Attachment 2). Information on submission of technical and price proposals is provided below. The proposal has written and oral components, as identified in Table 1 – Proposal Organization.

(B) Oral Proposal Medium: The Government intends to hold oral presentations virtually via a Microsoft (MS) Teams meeting. Offerors are not required to purchase any video teleconferencing (VTC) software in response to this solicitation and will not be reimbursed for any costs associated. Offerors must state in their submission whether they can or cannot present their oral proposal via MS Teams. If an offeror cannot present their oral proposal via MS Teams, the Government will make every reasonable attempt to accommodate the offeror via another VTC medium or voice-only telephone. By participating in the oral presentations, the offeror acknowledges that it complies with all solicitation rules and parameters, in accordance with applicable laws and statutes.

(C) Recording Oral Presentation of Proposal: Recording oral presentations by offerors or their representatives is strictly prohibited, notwithstanding local laws and regulations with regards to virtual meetings or voice-only telephone oral presentations. However, the Government plans to record oral presentations. In accordance with FAR 15.102(e), the Government will maintain an official record of the oral presentation, which will be the basis of the evaluation.

Oral Proposals: Directions for oral proposal elements are set forth below:

(a) See Table 1 in this RFP - Proposal Organization for Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach is to be proposed orally. Contractors shall submit slides of the planned oral proposal in accordance with the proposal due dates contained within this RFP. Slides shall be prepared using Microsoft PowerPoint, in any style the contractor deems reasonable; however, font size for slide text must be 14 points or larger. Font used for graphics shall be no smaller than ten points. All Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach, requirements must be included and discussed within the oral proposal to be considered complete. Slides shall be submitted at the same time proposals are due, in order to establish that a complete proposal has been submitted. It is further noted that the slides submitted with the written portions of the proposal MUST be the same slides used in the oral proposal. No additions or changes are permitted. The electronically submitted slides for the oral proposals will constitute the proposal submission for Factor 1, Subfactors 1 – 4 and shall contain all information to be covered in the oral proposal. There is a 36-page limit for the slides, and the proposal is limited to the time period identified below.

(b) Oral proposals are limited to a maximum of 60 minutes per contractor. Any information not briefed within the time limit will not be evaluated. At the conclusion of the proposal, there will be a 15-30 minute break followed by an estimated additional 30 minutes for a Question and Answer (Q&A) period to allow the Government to seek clarifications, if required. This dialogue is a component of the oral proposal and does not constitute discussions.

(d) Representation at the oral proposal is limited to four individuals per contractor. The four individuals shall consist of three speakers/presenters and one silent party driving the presentation. The three individuals that are speakers/presenters must include personnel who are being proposed to work on the effort and at least one individual must be either the individual who will be identified as key personnel upon award or proposing company's executive.

2. Oral Proposal. Oral proposals are limited to a maximum of 90 minutes per offeror. 3. Presentation Slides. Offerors must submit their presentation slides with their proposal submission for Subfactors 2.1 – 2.5 and must cover all presentation material. The slides must be received by the Contracting Officer via email no later than 48 hours prior to the scheduled presentation time. These slides will form part of the official record for evaluation. If the offeror does not include their slides, the Government will consider the offeror as non-responsive and exclude the offeror from further consideration. The presentation slides submitted must be the same slides used in the oral proposal. No additions or changes are permitted between the time the slides are submitted and the time of the oral proposal.

4. Oral Presentation Participants. Each offeror is allowed four representatives at the oral presentation: three speakers (three key personnel or two key personnel and one company executive) and one silent participant. These presentations will be conducted virtually via MS Teams DoD 365-J.

6. Term of Contract: The term of any resultant contract shall commence on the effective date indicated on page 1 and shall continue for a period of seven (7) years thereafter, unless terminated, cancelled or extended as otherwise provided herein.
7. Contract Extension: By mutual written contract amendment, any resultant contract may be extended for supplemental periods of up to a maximum of thirty-six (36) months.
8. Proposal Opening: Proposals shall be submitted no later than the due date and time indicated in the request for proposals. All information contained in the proposals shall be deemed as exempt from public disclosure based on the City's need to avoid disclosure of contents prejudicial to competing Offerors during the process of negotiation. The proposals shall not be open for public inspection until after contract award. PRICES SHALL NOT BE READ. After contract award, the successful proposal and the evaluation documentation shall be open for public inspection.
9. Discussions: In accordance with the City of Peoria Procurement Code, after the initial receipt of proposals, discussions may be conducted with Offerors who submit proposals determined to be reasonably susceptible of being selected for award.
10. The presentation shall be approximately 30 minutes, allowing 15 minutes for a question and answer session. The Consultant's Project/Team Manager shall lead the presentation team and answer questions on behalf of the Consultant. If work involves a major sub-consultant, the firms Project/Team Manager's presence may also be requested (by the City) at the interview.

SECTION 4 – PITCHES (PHASE 2)

4.1 General Guidelines

In Phase 2, companies shall provide an in-person pitch at their proposed location or other location agreed upon by the company and the Government to demonstrate and provide further details on their proposed concept / technology / solution and the technical and business viability of the proposed solution. The Government may also leverage external market research during Phase 2 to evaluate a company's viability.

In addition to the Pitch, the Government, at its discretion, may request for consideration an additional written submission to further supplement or clarify the information provided in the Phase 1 Solution Briefs. The Government, at its discretion, may elect to release additional information to companies that have been selected for the pitch.

The pitch format will be as follows:
(1) Up to a 90-minute presentation provided by the company, the Government may request an audio recording of this presentation
(2) Up to a 90-minute question and answer between the Government and company
(3) Up to a 120-minute site visit / tour of facilities by company, if the company location is selected

Individual pitches will be evaluated based on the merit of the proposed concept in addressing the SOW.

Q2:  Will the Government accept a migration plan submitted as an appendix to the slide presentation and that the migration services strategy plan be evaluated as part of the proposal?

A2:  No.  The offeror must present its approach to integrate with existing DISA solutions and provide the migration services (outlined in PWS section 6.1.8) as part of their oral presentation.  

Q3: Can offerors submit external handouts for network diagrams outside of their slide presentation and not count against the maximum slide count? 

A3: No. The offeror must present the network design as part of their oral proposal.  The offeror may include network diagrams of the proposed design within the presentation slides as visual aids.  These designs will count toward the maximum slide count. 

Q4: Would DISA consider Oral and Technical Proposal submitted separately? Meaning, can Orals be assigned once down selected? 

A4: No. Also, there is no down selection process outlined in the RFP.

Q5: Will the slides for the Technical and Management Approach (Volume II) be due at the same time as Price volume and other elements of the full proposal?

A5: Yes; see the addendum to FAR 52.212-1(a)(7)(vi), Electronic Offers.  The offeror must submit all volumes with their proposal. 

Q6: How will offerors schedule / be notified of their Oral Presentation date and time? 

A6: See addendum to 52.212-1(a)(7)(vii)(B)(7). The Government intends to schedule oral proposals with responsive offerors approximately 10 business days after the solicitation closes. Oral proposals will take place approximately within 30 days after they are scheduled; dates and times will be assigned at random for all responsive offerors.

Q7: Are all offerors given an opportunity to present their technical approach orally via MS Teams following timely and accurate submission of the proposal?

A7: If an offeror's proposal is timely and complete (adheres to the RFP), the Government will invite the offeror to present their technical proposal orally.

a. Subfactor 1: Offerors must brief a network design showcasing their approach to designing an enterprise network supporting multiple data centers as well as cloud environments utilizing the proposed solutions to meet the requirements of PWS Section 6.1 and PWS Appendix 5. The purpose of the network design is to demonstrate the offeror's approach to the proposed solution within the context of a Data Center network. The offeror may include network diagrams of the proposed design within the presentation slides as visual aids. Offerors may reference manufacturers' published data sheets for network components to demonstrate how solutions are meeting the requirements of PWS Appendix 5. The offeror must include the OEM data sheets for the devices used in the network design; the data sheets must be included as attachments/handouts to the presentation. The network design must use the criteria below.

(1) Design a network for two data centers, each with the following requirements:
(a) Traditionally hosted compute:
(i) Support for 1000 physical hosts per data center.
(ii) Support for 3000 virtual hosts.
(iii) Support 10Gbps throughput per host interface.
(iv) Future support of up to 100Gbps per host interface.
(v) Logical separation and control of Production and Management with further separation of Web, Database, App, Infrastructure Management, Enterprise Services, Data Replication, and Backup functions.
(vi) Solution must support micro segmentation and Zero Trust.
(vii) Support for fully architected enclaves requiring only external connectivity.
(2) Provide secure connectivity between two data centers.
(3) Provide secure connectivity between each data center and one or more commercial cloud environments.

2. Phase 2 – Oral Presentation/Pitch: Companies whose Solution Brief are evaluated to be of the most merit may be invited to provide a in-person pitch to provide further details of their proposed complete solution and the technical and business viability of the solution to PAE Fires subject matter expert panel, potentially consisting of representation across the PAE Fires and Army/DoD Community of Interest. The Government expects to limit the number of companies invited to pitch. As such, a company's solution may be evaluated to be of merit but not invited to pitch. Additionally, the Government may elect to proceed to Phase 3 without inviting any Companies to Pitch, or without receiving any Oral Presentations/Pitches.

Phase 2 – Oral Presentation/Pitch:
Individual pitches, if requested, are expected to provide additional insight into the Solutions Brief and as such will be evaluated against the same factors as the Solution Brief. Individual pitches will be initially evaluated on the basis of merit of the proposed concept in addressing the PWS and the factors above, not against any other pitches held under this announcement. 2.6 PROPOSAL EVALUATION
All proposals will be evaluated using the criteria specified in Section 4 of this RFP. Each proposal will be reviewed to determine whether it has been submitted in accordance with the proposal requirements. Proposals deemed non-responsive will be rejected from further review and the Offeror will be advised in writing.

Proposals deemed responsive will be evaluated based on the evaluation criteria set forth in this RFP. The County will determine which proposals are reasonably susceptible of being selected for award based on the evaluation criteria. The County may also conduct oral presentations/interviews.

Selection will include an analysis of proposals by an Evaluation Committee composed of County personnel who will review the proposal submittals in accordance with the submittal requirements and the evaluation criteria set forth in Section 4 of this RFP. The committee may request oral interviews and/or site visits. The County will determine which proposals are reasonably susceptible of being selected for award based on the evaluation criteria. Other factors, as detailed in the RFP, will be considered in determining what proposal will be deemed to best meet the needs of Fulton County. Awards will not necessarily be based on cost alone.

5.2.1.5 Finalist Presentation Evaluation

5.2.4 Total Proposal Score

The Solicitation Coordinator will calculate the sum of the Technical Proposal scores, the Presentation scores, and the Cost Proposal scores and record the resulting number as the total score for the subject Proposal.

2. Understanding of City Needs and System Integration 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated on their demonstrated understanding of the City's operational needs and objectives, as well as their ability to integrate with existing utility systems. This includes compatibility with GIS platforms, SCADA/ADMS data ingestion, CIS, AMI, and IVR interfaces, and the ability to maintain data accuracy, synchronization, and minimal latency across systems.

3. Implementation Methodology and Project Management 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated on the clarity and effectiveness of their implementation approach, including defined project phases and timeline, required utility resources, data migration and validation processes, and support for testing, cutover, and go-live activities to ensure a successful deployment.

4. Training, Support, and Lifecycle Services 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated on their ability to provide comprehensive initial and ongoing training, a clearly defined support structure with appropriate coverage, escalation procedures, and response times, and a proactive approach to software updates, enhancements, and cybersecurity.

B. Method of Award
This RFP is part of a competitive procurement process designed to serve the best interests of UPSTATE and the People of the State of New York. It is also designed to provide all bidders with a fair and even opportunity to have their services considered. UPSTATE will conduct a comprehensive review of each responsive bid submitted in accordance with the terms of this RFP. Proposals will be evaluated on the basis of "best value" by an evaluation committee comprised of UPSTATE representatives, and shall be conducted in the following steps:

3. Technical Evaluation (65 Points)- before presentation points
Each bid remaining after review of Bidder Qualifications will advance for review and independent evaluation and scoring by the technical review evaluation committee.

4. Financial Evaluation (25 Points)
The Financial Proposals of each bid remaining after the Technical Evaluation will be independently evaluated and scored by the designated contract officer.
Cost points will be awarded as follows: Lowest Total Contract Cost will receive twenty-five (25) points. All other bids will be awarded points based on a ratio of their cost to the lowest Total Contract Cost.

5. Presentation, Demonstration, Interview (10 Points)
Bidders that are susceptible to contract award based on their combined technical and cost score will be invited to provide a Presentation/Demonstration/Interview. UPSTATE will notify the Bidders and schedule the date and time.

6. Selection
Proposals will be evaluated on the basis of "best value" by an evaluation committee comprised of UPSTATE representatives. The Bidder earning the highest final composite score (technical, financial, and presentation/demonstration/interview vendor) will be selected to contract with UPSTATE.

DEMONSTRATION REQUIREMENTS (MODIFIED)
Demonstrations given by an Offeror under this section are permitted and communication by the Offeror with the Agency or its employees during a demonstration will not violate the restrictions applicable to Offerors. The presentation will be held virtually, and any expenses incurred by the Offeror are the Offeror's sole responsibility. The Procurement Officer will notify the responsive Offerors as quickly as possible to schedule a specific date and time.

THIS IS NOT A MARKETING OPPORTUNITY!

(a) The activities of the Offeror are limited to a demonstration of the system proposed and described in the Offeror's written proposal.
(b) Evaluators may ask questions pertaining to the Offeror's demonstration at the conclusion. The Offeror's answers are restricted to statements of fact. The Offeror may be required to document an answer if such a written clarification is determined to be in the best interest of the State.
(c) Offeror will not be allowed or permitted to introduce new information or show products/features not included in their proposal.
(d) Negotiation is not permitted at this stage in the procurement process.
(e) The demonstration should be conducted in a straightforward manner in order to secure a clear and meaningful understanding of the Offeror's proposal.

2.2.2.2 Subfactor 2b: Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation

Product Demonstration/Oral presentations shall be provided using Microsoft PowerPoint. The number of slides should not exceed 45. Offerors are instructed to submit their PowerPoint presentation with their proposal. Offerors will not have an opportunity to revise the PowerPoint after the submission due date has closed.

Offerors will be contacted about scheduling the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation. The Government anticipates the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation to take place within two (2) weeks from the proposal submission due date. The Government reserves the right to reschedule the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation at the sole discretion of the Contracting Officer. Government attendees will be identified at the time of the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation. If an Offeror fails to schedule the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation as instructed, the Offeror's proposal will not be evaluated and will make the Offeror ineligible for award.

The location for the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation will be at Joint Base Andrews, Maryland or Joint Base Anacostia-Bolling, DC. The Government reserves the right to change the site at the sole discretion of the Contracting Officer. Offerors may not present any classified information in either their proposals or their Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation.

During the Offeror's Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation, they will be given time to set up and then be allowed 60 minutes to demonstrate the live capabilities of their product. Offerors are instructed to demonstrate the objectives labeled 'presentation' in Table 2 above.

Presentations must be done in person and no more than two presenters per Offeror. Offerors shall not use any type of video during the oral presentation, whether as pre-recorded presentations or as part of the Offeror's briefing materials. No external consultants will be allowed to represent an Offeror in the product demonstration.

Note 1: The Government reserves the right to ask questions after the 60-minute presentation following a break for consultation. The Questions & Answers (Q&A) session will be considered in the Offeror's technical evaluation. Q&A does not constitute formal discussions.

Note 2: IAW RFO 15.202(a)(3), Oral Presentations will be recorded. If an Offeror refuses to have their Oral Presentation recorded, they will be determined Unacceptable and not further evaluated.

STEP 2 Evaluation

Offerors invited to Step 2 will be evaluated through technical evaluation and price. No other criteria will be used. Technical evaluation is more important than price.

Technical
The Government will evaluate the offeror's solution proposed during its demonstration, including presentation material, against the evaluation criteria described below. The Government will use a Pass/Fail system for the first two evaluation criteria. If the Government renders the first two factors for the proposed solution as "Pass," the proposal will be evaluated against criteria three through five below, which are in descending order of importance. Each demonstration and supporting material shall be evaluated on its individual merits rather than on a comparative basis with other offerors.

Technical Factors:
• The proposed solution is relevant and directly addresses the problem statement, objectives, and requirements outlined in the selected Focus Area. (Pass/Fail)
• The offeror's proposed data rights assertions adequately meet the Government's stated Focus Area data-rights intent. (Pass/Fail)
• The extent to which the proposed solution possesses strong technical merit, is feasible, and is supported by a clear and credible plan for identifying, managing, and mitigating technical and programmatic risks.
• The extent to which the offeror demonstrates company viability, possesses a qualified management team, and has provided a sound and acceptable notional schedule for project execution.
• The extent to which the solution presents a unique, underutilized, or innovative application of commercial technology, products, or practices that provides a compelling advantage over traditional approaches.

4.3 Pitch Basis of Evaluation
Individual pitches will be evaluated against the following factors:
(1) Relevance of the solution in addressing the proposed portions of the SOW and the requirements in this document;
(2) The solution has technical merit, adequately addresses Government need and is feasible;
(3) Evaluation of company viability;
(4) Company's approach is a compelling solution to the Government problem;
(5) The cost estimate and details behind this estimate;
(6) A notional production schedule is acceptable; and
(7) The potential impact of data rights assertions.

Upon completion of the Pitch phase evaluations, the Government will notify the Company that:
(1) the proposed concept/technology/solution has been selected for award of an OTA; or
(2) the proposed concept/technology/solution is not of interest to the Government.

SECTION 5 – PROTOTYPE PROJECT AWARDS (PHASE 3)

5.1 General Guidelines
After award of the OTA, the Government may issue the Company a Request for Project Proposal (RPP) for a project-specific proposal in accordance with a Collaborative Task Instruction (CTI). Receipt of an RPP does not guarantee that a Company will receive a prototype project award. The Government reserves the right, at any point prior to award, to cancel the RPP.

If an RPP is provided, the Company will be invited to develop and submit its proposal. Companies may discuss ideas and details of the proposal during the proposal writing process with the Government. Specifics on a CTI will be provided at the time of RPP.

Upon favorable review and available funds, the Government may choose to make a prototype project award to the Agreement Holder.

I.1. Summary. HQ SACT is issuing this Request for Innovative Participation (RFIP) announcement in order to facilitate collaboration between the HQ SACT, SHAPE (Supreme Headquarters Allied Powers Europe) and participants regarding solutions. The purpose of this RFIP is to request participants to submit solutions (either existing and/or under development) to the scenario presented within Annex I and so participate in the NATO Innovation Challenge 2026-1 (iChall26-1). All submissions must conform to this RFIP.

A Selection Committee composed of representatives from HQ SACT and other relevant experts invited by HQ SACT (the "reviewers") will select up to 10 solutions (hereinafter as "the Finalists") amongst the responses submitted. The Finalists will participate in the iChall26-1 Pitch Day projected to be May 28, 2026, in Rennes, FRANCE. Participation in the Pitch Day is only open to in-person. During the Pitch Day, a Jury composed of representatives from HQ SACT and other relevant experts invited by HQ SACT (the "Jury Members") will assess which of the 10 solutions best respond to the scenario presented in Annex I and select the iChall26-1 winners.

I.2. Dates. The event and timelines of iChall26-1 are described in Section III. It will take place between 10 March and 28 May 2026, with the iChall26-1 Pitch Day taking place on-site on 28 May 2026.

Criteria for Selection
Responsive proposals submitted in response to this RFP will be evaluated by a committee comprised of regional tourism partners and the Corporation's staff. Each committee member will independently review and score proposals based on the Evaluation Criteria set forth below. Following individual scoring, the committee will convene to discuss the proposals and determine the highest-scoring proposals.

The proposals receiving the highest total scores may be invited, at the Corporation's discretion, to participate in an oral presentation. The Corporation anticipates that up to three top-ranked Proposers may be asked to provide an oral presentation to the committee, at their own expense, at the Corporation's office in Providence, Rhode Island. Presentations may alternatively be conducted virtually at the sole discretion of the Corporation. In-person presentations shall include no more than three representatives from the Proposer's team, including management and key personnel responsible for day-to-day implementation.

Proposers will be asked to demonstrate the dashboard product and a representative dataset that supports the Corporation's tourism strategic planning efforts. A question-and-answer period will follow each presentation. Oral presentations are intended to clarify and respond to questions regarding the written proposals but will not affect the overall final score of the written proposals. Final scores and rankings will be based on the Committee's evaluation of the written proposals alone.

Oral Proposals: Directions for oral proposal elements are set forth below:

(a) See Table 1 in this RFP - Proposal Organization for Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach is to be proposed orally. Contractors shall submit slides of the planned oral proposal in accordance with the proposal due dates contained within this RFP. Slides shall be prepared using Microsoft PowerPoint, in any style the contractor deems reasonable; however, font size for slide text must be 14 points or larger using Arial, Times New Roman, or Courier New font. Font used for graphics, footer and page numbers shall be no smaller than ten points. Any other text present on any and all slides shall be 14-point font. Contractors may use both text slides and graphics slides. All Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach, requirements must be included and discussed within the oral proposal to be considered complete. Slides shall be submitted at the same time proposals are due, in order to establish that a complete proposal has been submitted. It is further noted that the slides submitted with the written portions of the proposal MUST be the same slides used in the oral proposal. No additions or changes are permitted. Contractors shall not include narrative discussions (i.e., note pages, handouts, or other similar narrative material) with the slides. The electronically submitted slides for the oral proposals will constitute the proposal submission for Factor 1, Subfactors 1 – 4 and shall contain all information to be covered in the oral proposal. There is a 36-page limit for the slides, and the proposal is limited to the time period identified below. This limitation does not include a cover slide or an agenda/summary slide.

(b) Oral proposals are limited to a maximum of 60 minutes per contractor. Any information not briefed within the time limit will not be evaluated. At the conclusion of the proposal, there will be a 15-30 minute break followed by an estimated additional 30 minutes for a Question and Answer (Q&A) period to allow the Government to seek clarifications, if required. This dialogue is a component of the oral proposal and does not constitute discussions.

SECTION M - EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

M.1. EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

a. The House intends to make one or more awards to Offerors whose proposals meet the requirements as stated in this solicitation. This will be a multi-phase evaluation. For the first phase proposals will be evaluated based on the following evaluation factors:

i. technical approach
ii. management approach
iii. corporate capabilities
iv. past performance
v. price.

b. Risk Assessment. Price proposals will be evaluated during phase 1 to identify and assess potential risks, which may be inherent in the Offeror's approach.

c. Price Realism. Proposed pricing will be evaluated during phase 1 not only to determine if the price is reasonable and affordable but may also be evaluated to determine if the pricing is realistic and reflects an understanding of the requirements.

d. Phase 2 Evaluation:

i. Oral presentations – Offerors whose proposals are considered to be in the competitive range from the phase 1 evaluations may be invited to participate in oral presentations. No new information shall be presented during this phase, these are not to be considered discussions, although clarifications may be provided. The competitive range may include a sufficient number of proposals to present the best value to the Government.

ii. Price – Offerors whose proposals are considered to be in the competitive range from the phase 1 evaluations, with or without an oral presentation, may be asked to revise their pricing to a "Best and Final Offer".

After evaluation of written proposals oral presentations are anticipated, however the House reserves the right to award without oral presentations if the written proposals are determined to be a sufficient discriminator of ability.

On-Site Oral Presentation Instructions

The On-Site Oral Presentation slide deck should follow a similar outline, and contain similar information, as the OPP. A template is not provided for the Oral Presentation slide deck, but a strong emphasis on the innovative claims, technical approach, and discussion of technical risks and mitigation strategies is encouraged. A section on how the proposed solution will achieve the program metrics is required. Slide deck content should include, but is not limited to:

Proposed System Description: Provide a top-level description of the proposed system to achieve the program metrics identified in Section 2.4. Describe the current state of technical maturity and identify all major analytical and test activities/tasks anticipated to demonstrate the technology has validity to expand into operational use.

Technology Challenges: Identify specific approaches to solving the technology challenges identified in Section 2.3.6. in order to achieve the stated program metrics. Identify the primary technical and programmatic risks to be addressed during conceptual design versus in detailed design and test or future efforts.

Technical Ability: Detail the specific qualifications and experience that position the Proposer's organization and proposed team to successfully achieve the goals of the CyPhER Forge program. The Proposer may include past experience, organizational capabilities, team members' qualifications, or other information that demonstrates competence in designing, building and testing the CyPhER Forge program.

Schedule and Price: Provide an outline of tasks in logical order that are required to achieve conceptual design maturity as stated in the PS. These should also be depicted in a schedule format showing duration of each major task and subtask. Include any proposed solution(s) to program security requirements unique to the CyPhER Forge program.

Phase 3 – Emulation Event / Proposal: Following the evaluation of Solution Briefs (and oral presentation/pitches, if applicable):
• For the Tomahawk Variant (W50RAJ269A004):
The Government may invite one or more offerors with the most meritorious solutions to participate in an emulation event.

The Government may, at its discretion, elect to issue RFPPs prior to, simultaneously with, following, or without an Emulation Event / Proposal invitation.

M.2 Evaluation of Factors
• Factor 1: Technical
o Subfactor 1.1: Written Technical Approach: The Government will evaluate the offeror's written proposal to assess the degree to which it demonstrates a clear understanding of the PWS requirements and presents a sound, feasible, and comprehensive approach. For Track 2 offerors, this evaluation will be limited to the PWS sections they have proposed.
o Subfactor 1.2: Oral Presentation: For offerors in the competitive range, the Government will evaluate the Key Personnel's performance during the scenario-based challenges. The evaluation will assess:
▪ The team's ability to quickly comprehend complex problems.
▪ The quality, creativity, and viability of the proposed solution.
▪ The team's collaborative process and communication skills.
▪ The demonstrated expertise of the Key Personnel.
▪ Effective use of any Government-provided tools (such as USAi) to enhance their solution. The results of the Oral Presentation will be used to validate or revise the initial rating of the Written Technical Approach, resulting in a single, consolidated rating for the Technical factor where innovation, commitment to extreme ownership, and effective use of artificial intelligence, are plus factors.

L.4 Phase II - Oral Presentations (Flyoff)
After evaluation of the written proposals, the Government will establish a competitive range in accordance with FAR 15.306(c). Offerors included in the competitive range will be invited to participate in an Oral Presentation, or "flyoff."
• Purpose: The Oral Presentation is intended to validate the offeror's proposed technical approach and allow the Government to assess the problem-solving and communication skills of the proposed Key Personnel.
• Participants: The presentation must be led by the proposed Key Personnel identified in Volume I.
• Format: During the session, the Government will present the offeror's team with one or more scenario-based challenges directly related to the PWS. The team will be given a defined period to collaborate and develop a solution, which they will then present to the Government evaluation panel.

M.3 Award Decision
The Government will make awards to the offerors representing the best value within each track.
• Track 1 (Unrestricted): The Government will rank the proposals based on the evaluation factors. The Source Selection Authority (SSA) will select the top-ranked proposals that represent the best value to the Government.
• Track 2 (Small Business Set-Aside): The Government will separately rank the proposals within this track. The SSA will select the top-ranked proposals that represent the best value to the Government.

M.1 Basis for Award
This is a competitive best value tradeoff source selection. The evaluation process will be conducted in two phases. Phase I will consist of the evaluation of the written proposals. Phase II will consist of Oral Presentations for those offerors determined to be in the competitive range. The final evaluation will incorporate the results of both phases.
Factor 1 (Technical) is significantly more important than Factor 2 (Past Performance). Factors 1 and 2, when combined, are significantly more important than Factor 3 (Cost/Price). As the non-cost/price factors become more equal among offerors, the importance of Cost/Price will increase.

Basis of award:
Award shall be made to the responsible proposer whose overall technical and financial proposal was evaluated as best meeting the needs of the University as set forth in the RFP.
Proposals received in response to this solicitation will be evaluated by MSU using source selection procedures. The award will be based upon an assessment of each offeror's ability to satisfy the solicitation requirements. MSU will make award to that responsible offeror conforming to the solicitation that is most advantageous to the university, price and other factors considered. An award may be made to other than the offeror who offers the lowest price proposal or to other than the best technical proposal but, who offers the best value to the university.

Relative Importance:
Award will be made to the offeror whose technical proposal and price provide the best value to the university. Factor 1: (Technical Approach) is more important than Factor 2: (Management Approach), Factor 2: (Management Approach) is more important than Factor 3: (Oral Presentation), Factor 3: (Oral Presentation) is more important than Factor 4: (Past Performance). As a whole, Factors 1, 2, 3, and 4 are more important than Factor 5 (Price).

3.2. Evaluation Stages
The proposal evaluation process will lead to the identification of a finalist or finalists and will take place in two stages:
1. Evaluation of Proposal responses;
2. Interviews/Equipment and Service demonstration.

Upon completion of each stage in the process, the Committee will score each Proposer and select those who are short listed for the subsequent stage.

3.3.2. Stage 2, Interviews/Demonstrations.
A short-list of firms may be invited to Stage 2, Interview/Demonstration, step of the evaluation process. Interview/demonstrations are an important aspect of the evaluation process that offers the City an opportunity to see how the Proposer's solution meets the critical components of the RFP. Evaluation of Respondents in Stage 2 consists of oral interviews and presentations by Proposers short listed in Stage 1, along with an in-person equipment demonstration and testing of proposed services. One hundred (100) points will be the maximum score for Stage 2. Interviews and presentations may be conducted in-person, virtual, or some combination thereof. Proposers invited to participate in Stage 2 will be given additional information regarding the City's desired content in a reasonable time before the scheduled Stage 2 oral interviews and presentations are held.

For equipment and service demonstration, Proposers shall provide at least two (2) vehicles for each type of Fleet Vehicle offered for the proposed service. Evaluation standards include verification of the Proposers' ability to deliver proposed services with available equipment, and verification of the Proposers' proposed solutions to provide equitable access to qualifying users.

1. Equipment Demonstration: The mobile phone app, RFID card, keypad, text-to-unlock and/or any other method for reserving, unlocking and/or holding must be functional and presented for testing by the Committee to be considered as part of the Equipment Demonstration score.

Compliance with the City's safety requirements and any proposed safety technology, including, but not limited to, geofencing, speed governing, and sidewalk detection features, must be functional and presented for testing by the Evaluation Committee and will be considered as part of the Equipment Demonstration score.

If the Proposer includes any new or enhanced parking technology in its proposal, a demonstration of such technology must be provided as part of the Equipment Demonstration.

If the Proposer proposes features such as audible or visual warnings, detection of sidewalk riding, detection of multiple riders on a single device, or similar rider behavior monitoring technologies, these features must be demonstrated during the Equipment Demonstration.

The Equipment Demonstration factors will include riding on different surfaces, flat ground, inclines, accelerating, stopping, turning, starting, stopping, handlebar height adjustments, and other riding factors.

52.222-50 (DEV) Combating Trafficking in Persons (Oct 2025) (DEVIATION DEC 2025)
(a) Definitions. As used in this clause-
Agent means any individual, including a director, an officer, an employee, or an independent contractor, authorized to act on behalf of the organization.
Coercion means-
(1) Threats of serious harm to or physical restraint against any person;
(2) Any scheme, plan, or pattern intended to cause a person to believe that failure to perform an act would result in serious harm to or physical restraint against any person; or
(3) The abuse or threatened abuse of the legal process.
Commercial sex act means any sex act on account of which anything of value is given to or received by any person.
Commercially available off-the-shelf (COTS) item —
(1) Means any item of supply (including construction material) that is—
(i) A commercial product (as defined in paragraph (1) of the definition of "commercial product" at Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 2.101;
(ii) Sold in substantial quantities in the commercial marketplace; and
(iii) Offered to the Government, under a contract or subcontract at any tier, without modification, in the same form in which it is sold in the commercial marketplace; and
(2) Does not include bulk cargo, as defined in 46.U.S.C. 40102(4), such as agricultural products and petroleum products.
(b) Policy. The United States Government has adopted a policy prohibiting trafficking in persons including the trafficking-related activities of this clause. Contractors, contractor employees, and their agents must not—
(1) Engage in severe forms of trafficking in persons during the period of performance of the contract;
(2) Procure commercial sex acts during the period of performance of the contract;
(3) Use forced labor in the performance of the contract;

2. Best Value Criteria
The Cooperative evaluates Proposals on the basis of best value to Cooperative members. In evaluating Proposals and determining best value for all Cooperative members, the Cooperative will consider the following criteria:

Best Value Criteria Maximum Points
1 Price Competitiveness 45
2 Vendor Past Performance 15
3 Vendor Reputation for Goods and Services 15
4 Financial and Technical Resources 15
5 Capability of Servicing Cooperative Members 5
6 Any other relevant factor or requirement listed in this Proposal Invitation 5
TOTAL 100

The Cooperative's evaluation may include Vendor's responses to the forms and other attachments or information included with a Proposal or associated with this Proposal Invitation, including but not limited to Vendor's responses to the Proposal Invitation Questionnaire, as well as any other information at its disposal deemed relevant by the Cooperative in its sole discretion. Only responsive Proposals will be evaluated. Failure to include all required information may result in a Proposal being deemed non-responsive.

As a general matter, the Cooperative seeks to make competitive and indefinite quantity awards to Proposers that give the same or better discounts/pricing than they give their best governmental clients and can provide best value to Cooperative members.

1. EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD – GENERAL PROCEDURES
A. The Government will evaluate proposals using a tradeoff process to determine which offerors represent the Best Value to the Government, as defined in FAR 2.101 and described in FAR 52.212-2. The Government may award to other than the lowest priced offeror or to other than the proposal assessed the most non-price merit. The relative importance considering all evaluation factors is provided as follows: all evaluation factors other than cost or price, when combined, are significantly more important than cost or price.

B. The Government estimates up to four (4) awards will be made under this solicitation. This estimate in no way prohibits the Government from making either fewer or more awards than estimated based upon the offers received and a determination of the best interests of the Government.

C. The Government may award without discussions with Offerors in accordance with FAR 52.215-1. Therefore, the Offeror's initial proposal should contain the Offerors' best terms from a technical and price standpoint. The Government reserves the right to conduct discussions if deemed necessary.

One phone on inmate side per booth
Two phones on visitor side per booth

Phone access requires entering a pin each time.

Provide a minimum of one phone in visiting with the ability to amplify the sound for individuals with hearing impairments.

Laminated directions shall be placed by each phone with directions on phone use. CONTRACTOR will replace inmate instructions when there is a change.

Visitation phones times are set for 30 minutes maximum call time but this duration can be changed for special visits by staff.

Visitation phones provide a 2 minute warning prior to the phone shutting off and a 30 second warning prior to the phone shutting off.

Once visitation is complete the phone has auto shut off and visitor/inmate cannot continue to communicate.

Provide your backup plan to allow visitation if phone system is non-functioning.

This is a competitive solicitation seeking proposals for innovative, commercial technologies that accelerate attainment of defense capabilities. It is intended that this solicitation will result in the award of prototype projects, which may include not only commercially available technologies fueled by commercial or strategic investment, but also concept demonstrations, pilots, and agile development activities that can incrementally improve and rapidly field commercial technologies, existing Government-owned capabilities, or concepts for defense application.

This document outlines the phased competitive procedures for submitting proposals to this solicitation:

Phase 1: Solution Briefs – Solution Briefs submitted in response to this solicitation shall be submitted as specified in Section 3.2 of this document and will be evaluated against the criteria stated in this solicitation. The Government will not pay companies for the costs associated with Solution Briefs development and submission.

Phase 2: Pitches – Companies submitting qualified Solution Briefs may be invited to Phase 2, which is to provide a pitch which must comply with the instructions detailed in Section 4.0 of this document.

SECURITY REQUIREMENTS FOR U.S. PASSPORT PRINTER & MAINTENANCE CONTRACT

The Bureau of Diplomatic Security (DS), U.S. Department of State (DoS), reserves the right and authority to conduct periodic inspections of contractor facilities and to perform background investigations on the company and its management in terms of its affiliations with persons or organizations that are inimical to the security interests of the United States, as well as background investigations of company employees and inspection of contractor facilities that are necessary to ensure proper compliance with the terms of the contract. The particular area of security interest under this contract relates to the manufacturing facilities and its employees who will be involved in the manufacturing of the printers and/or the maintenance of the printers during their life cycle.

A TOP SECRET facility clearance is required for the Prime Contractor at the beginning of contractor performance.

Facility and Personnel Security Clearances

(a) A facility security clearance at the TOP SECRET level, issued by the Defense Counterintelligence and Security Agency (DCSA), is required for performance on this contract and any subsequent task orders in accordance with the DD Form 254, Department of Defense Contract Security Classification Specification.

(b) Since it will be necessary for some Contractor personnel to have access to classified material and/or to enter into areas requiring a security clearance, each Contractor employee requiring such access must have an individual security clearance issued by DCSA commensurate with the required access prior to performance. Individuals must maintain their personnel security clearance throughout the duration of employment under this award. At a minimum, personnel performing on-site maintenance at DoS locations must have a SECRET personnel clearance.

SCOPE

This Performance Work Statement (PWS) broadly defines the requirements for Engineering Services and Science Capability Augmentation (ESSCA) II provided to the Marshall Space Flight Center (MSFC) in Huntsville, Alabama; the Stennis Space Center (SSC) in Hancock County, Mississippi; and the Michoud Assembly Facility (MAF) in New Orleans, Louisiana, which is managed by MSFC. Throughout the entirety of the contract, all references to MSFC are inclusive of MAF. In addition, this contract may support NASA Centers including Armstrong Flight Research Center (AFRC), Ames Research Center (ARC), Glenn Research Center (GRC), Goddard Space Flight Center (GSFC), National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Headquarters (HQ), Johnson Space Center (JSC), Kennedy Space Center (KSC), and Langley Research Center (LaRC).

The ESSCA II requirement is a follow-on to the ESSCA contract. Services and skills are to be provided across a broad spectrum of engineering and science disciplines and requested support will include, but not be limited to, research, design, development, analysis, testing, operations, and sustaining engineering.

The ESSCA II contract will provide engineers, scientists, and engineering technicians in support of MSFC and other Centers, as well as programs, projects, and other activities across the Agency, both present and future. The contract also provides scope for end-item deliverables. The contract includes support for facilitating partnerships and identifying new pursuits and opportunities that align with MSFC strategy. ESSCA II will also support reimbursable work for which MSFC, and other Centers, are responsible. This work can include support to the Department of Defense (DoD), other Government agencies, commercial industry, and academia.

FAA Answer: The existing ATO will transfer to new awardee.

CyPhER Forge is a 30-month, three (3) phase program designed to rapidly mitigate technical risk, drive innovation through competition, and mature the core technologies across six (6) technical pillars.

Phase 0 Backbone (planned for 6 months) builds the program's Integration, Validation, and Verification infrastructure.

Phase 1 Base (planned for 12 months) enables rapid risk reduction with challenge cycles to score performance in accuracy and speed for modeling, data assimilation, and uncertainty quantification as evaluated against HFMs and hold-out flight test data from experimental aircraft. Proposers achieving top technical performance across program metrics, as demonstrated in Phase 1 Base challenge cycles, will be selected to continue to perform in Phase 2 Option.

Phase 2 Option (planned for 18 months) is targeted to mature and integrate the DTs and AI Test Agents. The challenge cycles in Phase 2 Option will provide quantitative evidence of the Proposers' abilities to develop a real-time, knowledge and safety maximizing AI Test Agents and will serve as the primary readiness criteria for flight tests. Phase 2 Option will culminate in a full-scale demonstration wherein the integrated CyPhERs will autonomously execute a test plan on an experimental aircraft.

This PS encourages solutions from all responsible sources capable of satisfying the requirements as identified in the PS, including large and small businesses, nontraditional defense contractors as defined in 10 U.S.C. § 3014, and research institutions as defined in 15 U.S.C. § 638.

DARPA's acquisition strategy for the CyPhER Forge program is structured to minimize the administrative burden of entry, reduce program risk, foster competition, and enable performing teams to begin work faster.

Task 2.2 – Prototype Implementations on Small-Scale Models
The Subcontractor shall:
• Implement reference versions of at least two to three core neuroscience-aligned objectives using:
  o Public LLMs and/or moderate-scale variants, and
  o Lab and/or publicly available brain datasets.
• Demonstrate that:
  o The objectives are numerically stable; and
  o They improve alignment metrics defined in Task 1 (e.g., encoding correlation, ISC) compared with simple baselines.
Deliverable T2.2: Prototype code modules (e.g., PyTorch / HuggingFace style) implementing selected objectives, with example training scripts and a short results note (figures or summary tables).
Task 2.3 – Integration Requirements for NeuroX Training
The Subcontractor shall:
• Translate objective designs into clear API and data requirements for the NeuroX training stack, specifying:
  o Necessary internal activations (e.g., layer-wise embeddings, temporal pooling outputs).
  o Annotation formats and batching schemes for aligned brain–text–video windows.
• Coordinate with BNL and external collaborators (e.g., Cha/Moon labs) so these objectives are compatible with other knowledge-constrained loss functions and multi-modal fusion layers planned for NeuroX.
Deliverable T2.3: "NeuroX Training Integration Requirements" (PDF), optionally co-signed with BNL, for engineering implementation.

1.8. ELIGIBLE PROPOSERS
All responsible sources capable of satisfying the Government's needs may submit a proposal in response to this ISO, except as noted below. Specifically, universities, non-profit organizations, small businesses and other than small businesses are eligible and encouraged to propose to this ISO.

1.9. PROHIBITION OF PERFORMER PARTICIPATION FROM FEDERALLY FUNDED RESEARCH AND DEVELOPMENT CENTERS (FFRDCS) AND GOVERNMENT ENTITIES
ARPA-H is primarily interested in responses to programs from commercial performers, academia, non-profit organizations, etc. In certain circumstances, FFRDCs and U.S. Government Entities will have unique capabilities that are not available to proposing teams through any other resource. Accordingly, the following principles will apply to this ISO:
(1) FFRDCs and U.S. Government entities, including federal Government employees, are not permitted to respond to this ISO as a prime or sub-performer on a proposed performer team.
(2) If an FFRDC or U.S. Government entity has a unique research idea that is within the technology scope of this ISO that they would like considered for funding, contact this email address: CIRCLE@arpa-h.gov.
(3) If an FFRDC or U.S. Government entity, including a federal Government employee, is interested in working directly with the Government team supporting the research described by this ISO, the party should contact CIRCLE@arpa-h.gov.

3.2.5 Help Desk Operation/IT Support

The Contractor shall provide a capacity to maintain a help desk operation to assist users with computer-related problems. The help desk receives requests via electronic mail, a telephone "hot line", or personal visit. This capability can be addressed informally via the various on-site Contractor staff; however, it must accommodate the staggered working hours that are commonplace in the SSD, typically 7 AM to 7 PM, Monday through Friday. The Contractor shall respond to user requests as well as prioritize, track, report, and analyze these requests. The tasks required by the help desk may include, but are not limited to: assisting users when system-related problems arise, creating and maintaining administrative user training and help documentation; and troubleshooting and diagnosing problems, such as system failures, determining whether the source is hardware, system software, or applications software related and providing customers with GFE backup systems to perform work in the event of catastrophic failures if system-related problems cannot be resolved in a timely fashion.

The Contractor shall provide briefing/presentations and meeting support to include (a) setup and maintenance of IT and IT-related hardware such as video projectors and laptops, (b) software help, and (c) information technology services for SSD meetings.

This procurement is for the proprietary, commercial off-the-shelf (COTS), brand-name Menlo Security, Inc.'s Cloud Browser (MSCB) product, as a Menlo Security, Inc. reseller-provided managed service, providing the Department of War's (DoW's) remote browsing isolation (RBI) solution for CBII. The action includes the renewal of existing subscription licenses, the procurement of new subscription licenses, and the incorporation of Menlo Security, Inc.'s Highly Evasive and Adaptive Threats (HEAT) Shield with Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning (AI/ML) capabilities to enhance cybersecurity measures.

Since 2020, the DoW has leveraged CBII through the MSCB platform under a production Other Transaction Authority (OTA) agreement. Over the past five years, MSCB has continuously evolved to safeguard DoW users from cyber threat actors by proactively engineering defenses against malicious tactics, techniques, and procedures.

Originally hosted on Amazon Web Services, this capability has been further enhanced with the strategic expansion of MSCB to a second cloud environment, the Google Cloud Platform. This expansion improves system redundancy, optimizes load balancing, and ensures uninterrupted availability of the CBII environment, even during outages. MSCB guarantees continuous, secure web browsing for DoW personnel worldwide.

The CBII Program Management Office (PMO) projects the total user base under OPORD 21-0006 to range between 3M and 3.2M, with the capacity to scale up to 3.4M users to accommodate surge requirements under an enterprise licensing model.

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT
DATASCRIBE DUPLICATION SYSTEM SUPPORT
23 December 2025
1. DESCRIPTION OF SERVICES. The contractor shall furnish labor, travel, parts, and material necessary to provide scheduled and non-scheduled maintenance of the DataScribe Duplication System to the 402d Software Engineering Group (SWEG), Robins Air Force Base, GA. Services required include equipment maintenance, software and any needed upgrades.

1.1 Scheduled Maintenance: The contractor shall perform Preventive Maintenance (PM) four (4) times per year at three (3) month intervals and shall contact the Government representative to establish the date and time to perform the PM operation.

1.1.1 The contractor and System Manager (SM) shall coordinate the preventive maintenance schedules. The contractor shall submit to the SM on an annual basis a tentative maintenance schedule for the life of the contract. PM will include, but is not limited to, system software upgrades, licensing issues, optimizing system software, making corrective adjustments as deemed necessary to keep the system in normal operating condition, lubrication, cleaning and making corrective adjustments, and replacing maintenance parts as deemed necessary to keep the system in good operating condition. Field engineering changes shall be installed during PM and any problems that have occurred since last PM shall be corrected.

The primary goal of this Request for Proposal (RFP) is to acquire the services of a Vendor able to provide a modern security video camera management system complete with adequate storage and viewing capabilities both on-premises and via Cloud. The scope of work required under this RFP includes the procurement of security cameras and associated equipment (including software), design, installation, implementation, configuration, testing and training. The selected Vendor will be responsible for recommending the type and number of security cameras, and any other applicable equipment, hardware and software associated with the security cameras. The selected Vendor must ensure it has adequate resources to provide these services and to meet the needs of HACP's HOPE VI Family Rental Housing Community. The security video camera management system recommended by the Vendor must have the following features: Centralized management system and standardized access. Able to be viewed from a computer via a web browser and from a cellular telephone. Show historical video while recording, hold historical data for a minimum of 30 days and download video. Record on motion, adjust to daylight and nighttime vision. Monitor the entire identified area. Allow future growth of the system to accommodate additional cameras and locations. High-Definition cameras capable of viewing fine details.

TECHNICAL EVALUATION CRITERIA WEIGHT

CRITERION 1: TECHNICAL PLAN/APPROACH 45
Appropriateness, feasibility, and adequacy of the proposed technical plan/approach for accomplishing the tasks outlined in the Statement of Work and the overall objectives of the solicitation.

CRITERION 2: SCIENTIFIC AND TECHNICAL PERSONNEL 25
Appropriateness and adequacy of the education, training, experience, expertise, and proposed levels of effort of the Principal Investigator and scientific and technical staff including subcontractors/consultants for accomplishing the tasks outlined in the Statement of Work and the overall objectives of the solicitation.

CRITERION 3: PROJECT MANAGEMENT 20
Appropriateness and adequacy of the Project Management Plan in terms of staffing, organizational structure and lines of authority, management of subcontracts/consultants, tracking of project activities, monitoring progress and timelines, and communication with stakeholders.

CRITERION 4: FACILITIES, EQUIPMENT, AND OTHER RESOURCES 10

TOTAL POSSIBLE WEIGHT 100

PRE-AWARD SITE VISIT OR SITE AUDIT

Offerors determined, upon completion of the scientific/technical peer review, to be in the Competitive Range may be subject to a pre-award site visit or auditing of their facilities and Quality Assurance and Quality Control (QA/QC) capabilities. The decision to conduct a pre-award site visit or audit specific facilities will be made by the Contracting Officer's Representative. Offerors, including proposed subcontractors, will be requested to make all non-proprietary records, including previous regulatory inspection records, and staff available in response to a pre-award site visit or audit by the NIAID or its designee. Due to timeline requirements, pre-award site visits may be made with short notice. Offerors are expected to guarantee the availability of key staff or other staff determined by the Government as essential for purposes of this site visit.

PURPOSE

The purpose of this Request for Information (RFI) is to solicit information to establish current requirements for a web-based assessment tool. ECECD will gather information from qualified early childhood vendors, professionals, and PreK providers regarding comprehensive developmental assessment tools suitable for use in NM PreK programs. The information gathered during this process shall inform future procurement and implementation strategies that align with the state's vision for equitable, culturally responsive, and developmentally appropriate early learning. The RFI is for planning purposes only and does not obligate ECECD to issue a solicitation or award a contract.

The goal of this RFI is to identify high-quality, research-based, developmental assessment tools that:

Align with New Mexico's Early Learning Guidelines and NM PreK standards.

Support instructional planning and family engagement.

Provide actionable data for educators, administrators, and policymakers.

TARGET RESPONDENTS

ECECD invites responses to this RFI from:

Early childhood assessment vendors with proven tools for PreK populations.

Early childhood professionals with expertise in developmental screening and assessment.

PreK providers currently using or evaluating assessment tools in classroom settings.

5. SECTION B - SCOPE OF SERVICES:
5.1. Background
The Office of the County Attorney (OCA) provides legal advice and services to County departments, agencies, boards and commissions, as well as the County Council. Currently there are about 55 staff attorneys and 25 legal support staff to handle legal issues, and provide opinions, prepare and present legislation, negotiate and prepare agreements and other documents on behalf of the County, draft resolutions and ordinances, acquire and convey title to real property, and represent the County in court and before various administrative bodies.
As counsel to one of the largest and most populous Counties in Maryland, the Office of the County Attorney collects and generates huge amounts of electronic data on a daily basis. This includes the emails, documents and many other forms of electronically stored information ("ESI") that are often used in the discovery phase during litigation and investigations as well as for responding to Maryland Public Information Act ("MPIA") requests. Annually, OCA handles an average of 230 lawsuits and 60 Maryland Public Information Act requests.
OCA requires the capability to effectively manage and review ESI during litigation and to efficiently respond to MPIA requests.
5.2. Intent
OCA is seeking proposals from qualified vendors for the provision of a cloud-based electronic discovery ("e-discovery") Software as a Service (SaaS) solution that will allow OCA to collect, store, process, analyze, review and produce ESI as part of discovery in litigation or in response to MPIA requests.

Description of Action: This procurement is for VISN 17 Picture Archiving and Communication System (PACS) equipment and software maintenance and support services for the PACS currently in use throughout VISN 17. The proposed sole source task order will be a firm-fixed price order against the Defense Logistics Agency Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contract SPE2D1-18-D-0006 with Optum/Change Healthcare Technologies, 1 Optum CIR, Eden Prairie, Minnesota, 55344. The period of performance will be a twelve-month base period with one twelve-month option period. Description of Supplies or Services: The PACS Enterprise Imaging is an enterprise clinical system that holds all VISN 17 Facilities Radiology PACS images. It is used 24/7 for Radiology operations. Radiology Service utilizes a Change Healthcare PACS that utilizes an intranet-based proprietary software. This requirement will provide maintenance and support for the Radiology, the Cardiology Services and ensure interoperability and compatibility between both services. The maintenance and support for the systems and its various hardware cannot be provided by a third-party vendor as the software is proprietary to Change Healthcare. Change Healthcare is the only vendor that has access to the Change Healthcare data link layer (VLAN) that connects all current PACS workstations that are currently in use.
